message.content: Adversaries may exploit the Subvert Trust Controls technique by tampering with Subject Interface Packages (SIPs) and trust provider components to deceive the operating system and application control tools during signature validation checks. This manipulation can cause malicious code to be incorrectly classified as legitimately signed, thereby bypassing security policies that restrict execution to trusted code. The WinVerifyTrust API function, which coordinates with trust providers to validate digital signatures, is a key target. By hijacking SIPs—software components that abstract API functions for various file formats—attackers can enable persistent code execution and evade detection. Common exploitation methods include modifying or replacing SIP and trust provider components to mislead signature validation processes. Mitigation strategies involve enforcing strict code signing policies, monitoring for unauthorized changes to SIP and trust provider components, and employing advanced endpoint detection and response (EDR) solutions to detect and block suspicious activities related to signature validation.
